title: Defeat for Trump, victory for rule of law
sub_topics: Trump's three SCOTUS appointees (Kavanaugh, Gorsuch, Barrett)
Davide-Panganiban inhibition in Estrada v. Desierto
constitutional standards for judges (competence, integrity, probity, independence)

stances.claim: The SCOTUS 7-2 rejection of the Texas v. Pennsylvania petition — including unanimous concurrence from Trump's own three appointees — demonstrates that 'the rule of law is much alive in the US' and that the country can again be looked up to for free-world leadership.
stances.rhetorical_move: rhetorical victory framing + analogy to Philippine experience

stances.claim: Decisions and actions of government must conform to the Constitution and the law, not to the wiles and whims of the governors — this is how the rule of law works, and it is why Estrada's own appointees did not dissent from his ouster.
stances.rhetorical_move: rule-of-law definition stated through personal anecdote

stances.claim: Those who don the Court's black robes must undergo a transformation — forfeiting past activities, relatives, friends, especially the appointing president, and devoting full time to the exclusive and reclusive world of decision-making.
stances.rhetorical_move: judicial role-transformation principle stated as moral ideal

stances.claim: Presidents should be happy, not aggrieved, when their judicial appointees rule against them — that is the fulfillment of the sworn duty 'Fiat justitia ruat caelum.'
stances.rhetorical_move: Latin maxim + reframing of disappointment as ethical vindication

institutions: Supreme Court of the United States
events: January 6, 2021 Electoral College count (anticipated at time of writing)
EDSA II People Power and Estrada's ouster

signature_phrases: the wiles and whims of the governors
the exclusive and reclusive world of decision-making
Let justice be done though the heavens may fall
to don the Court's black robes
the hallowed doors of the Supreme Court
register_markers: I could not speak for his appointees, but I explained
As I have written in several books
decision_framework_signals: applies rule-of-law principle as universally binding across US and Philippine contexts
frames judicial-appointment loyalty as anti-judicial — appointees who don't side with appointers are exemplary, not disloyal
uses Estrada anecdote as personal teaching device for the rule-of-law lesson
cross_references: As I have written in several books
notable_anecdotes: Estrada asking him in a one-on-one meeting at Jose Pardo's Ayala Alabang home why none of Estrada's own SC appointees dissented from his ouster
Davide and Panganiban inhibiting from the 13-0 Estrada v. Desierto decision
